QGZ8ED.sentenceLength: Describe their type of company, including industry, size, operating model, and maturity — for example, “multi-product SaaS companies with 500–2,000 employees navigating platform consolidation”
QGZ8ED.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: multi-product SaaS companies with 500–2,000 employees navigating platform consolidation
B2B FinTech scale-ups with 300–800 employees preparing for a late-stage funding round
enterprise software companies with 1,000+ engineers managing portfolios across multiple business units
professional services firms with 500–1,500 people allocating shared delivery capacity across competing client engagements

examples: Meridian
Vertex Systems
your organization
Acme

PLPKVT.sentenceLength: Describe a concrete, environment-specific challenge using the exact language they would use internally. Avoid abstract terms like “alignment.” Instead, reference second-level contributors such as fragmented reporting structures, inconsistent prioritization criteria, duplicate data entry, unclear ownership, governance bottlenecks, etc.
PLPKVT.description: Important: this cannot be generic executive shorthand (for example, "alignment" or "visibility"). Name the second-order cause behind the pain: structural constraints, behavior patterns, or coordination dynamics that create the visible symptom.
examples: Product and engineering teams operate off separate roadmaps with no shared source of truth, so capacity commitments made in planning are invisible to the teams accountable for delivery

Initiative owners spend two to three days each quarter manually reconciling headcount data from Workday against Jira epics just to produce a planning readout that is already stale by the time it lands
Every cross-functional dependency gets caught late because there is no mechanism for teams to surface blockers upstream — the only forcing function is the weekly status call
Resource allocation decisions are made in isolation by department heads, so the company frequently over-invests in low-leverage work while strategic bets sit understaffed

F6LDG7.sentenceLength: Describe another specific operational or structural friction point, again in practical, lived language.
F6LDG7.description: Again, avoid generic labels. Describe the underlying mechanism in concrete terms: where handoffs break, where ownership gets fuzzy, where decision rules conflict, or where process design drives friction.
examples: Leaders cannot distinguish between teams that are underperforming and teams that were set up to fail — there is no shared record of what was committed, what changed, and why
The governance process adds three to four weeks to any meaningful strategic pivot because every reallocation requires a multi-stakeholder sign-off chain that was not designed for quarterly speed
Portfolio reviews surface financial variances but not the behavioral or structural dynamics causing them, so the same categories of waste recur cycle after cycle
When an initiative stalls, the default response is to add a program manager and a new reporting layer — which delays the real question of whether the work should continue at all

9YCMRV.sentenceLength: Describe a third grounded challenge that reflects real constraints or tradeoffs they experience.
9YCMRV.description: Keep this one equally specific. Focus on operational reality and tradeoffs, not broad symptom words. The reader should be able to picture the exact dynamic causing the issue.
examples: The planning process requires six weeks of preparation but produces commitments that teams stop following by week three of execution — the overhead is high and the half-life is short
There is no shared language between finance, product, and engineering for describing what the company is investing in, so every cross-functional conversation starts with a taxonomy debate
The people closest to the work rarely have the authority to surface trade-offs, while the people with authority rarely have the operational context to make good calls

Strategic priorities are documented at the executive level but never translated into specific resource commitments — so teams are 'aligned on strategy' but executing against completely different interpretations of it

M21EY8.sentenceLength: Describe the meta-challenge that all of these relate to
M21EY8.description: Be concrete and operational. Name the real mechanism causing the pain, not just a symptom word like alignment or visibility.
examples: the gap between how work gets decided and how it actually gets done
a structural disconnect between resource ownership and outcome accountability
the coordination tax that compounds every time strategic intent has to travel through too many layers before reaching the teams doing the work
the inability to make and revisit capacity decisions with enough speed and context to stay strategically coherent

0XNTDJ.sentenceLength: Meta-diagnosis of why these challenges remain
0XNTDJ.description: Be concrete and operational. Name the real mechanism causing the pain, not just a symptom word like alignment or visibility.
examples: the tools and processes used to plan work were designed for a slower, more predictable world — they have not kept pace with the speed at which strategy needs to adapt
accountability for outcomes and accountability for resources sit in different organizational structures with no shared language or interface between them
every attempt to fix the problem optimizes for a local symptom rather than the underlying coordination architecture
the people closest to the work rarely have the authority to surface trade-offs, while the people with authority rarely have the operational context to make good calls

1PKZRQ.sentenceLength: Describe the widely acknowledged industry chatter, hype, or trend in this space — for example, “AI copilots for strategy,” “enterprise agility 2.0,” “lean portfolio management,” etc.
1PKZRQ.description: Choose a trend the audience already recognizes and connect it to their actual operating context.
examples: AI copilots for strategy
enterprise agility 2.0
lean portfolio management
continuous planning and rolling forecasts
outcome-based operating models

491OAD.sentenceLength: Underlying hope that these buzzword ideas and discussions can solve the challenge
491OAD.description: Be concrete and operational. Name the real mechanism causing the pain, not just a symptom word like alignment or visibility.
examples: teams could operate with full autonomy while leaders retain genuine portfolio-level visibility and control
strategic priorities could be communicated once and automatically cascade into team-level plans without a layer of manual translation
organizations could have real-time clarity on where their capital is going and what it is actually producing — without waiting for a quarterly review
planning and execution could live in the same system so the gap between intent and action becomes auditable, not invisible

O5R1N5.sentenceLength: Describe current shifts affecting their environment — e.g., budget tightening, AI acceleration, regulatory pressure, distributed teams, market volatility
O5R1N5.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: budget tightening
AI acceleration
regulatory pressure

JYVHWE.sentenceLength: Explain concretely how those shifts intensify the original challenges — e.g., increase complexity, reduce margin for error, amplify coordination load, etc.
JYVHWE.description: Be concrete and operational. Name the real mechanism causing the pain, not just a symptom word like alignment or visibility.
examples: increase complexity
reduce margin for error
amplify coordination load
compress the window in which a good decision is still possible
raise the cost of misallocated capital at exactly the moment when that capital is hardest to replace

55H0Y3.sentenceLength: Describe how companies typically try to fix these problems — for example, adding another tool, layering governance, hiring PMO roles, creating reporting dashboards, etc.

examples: adding another tool

R061BJ.sentenceLength: Explain why those attempts fail — such as reinforcing silos, increasing process overhead, creating translation burdens, or lacking shared context

examples: reinforcing silos
increasing process overhead
creating translation burdens
lacking shared context
treating the symptom rather than the coordination architecture causing it
optimizing for the visibility of the reporting layer rather than the quality of the decisions it is supposed to inform

25VEKS.sentenceLength: Describe edge solutions or extreme workarounds — e.g., heavy centralization, radical autonomy, spreadsheet systems, custom internal tooling, etc.
25VEKS.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: heavy centralization
radical autonomy
spreadsheet systems
custom internal tooling
a dedicated planning ops team whose full-time job is maintaining the portfolio model by hand
a bi-weekly leadership war room that exists solely to manually reconcile conflicting priorities

7I2H0S.sentenceLength: Describe the new pains those approaches create — fragility, lack of scalability, loss of visibility, political friction, etc.
7I2H0S.description: Be concrete and operational. Name the real mechanism causing the pain, not just a symptom word like alignment or visibility.
examples: brittle processes that break the moment a key person leaves or the org chart changes
lack of institutional memory — every new planning cycle starts from scratch because nothing was captured in a durable, queryable form
the overhead of maintaining the workaround itself often exceeds the overhead it was supposed to eliminate
political friction when the workaround gives one team visibility into another team's prioritization decisions without a shared framework for interpreting what they see

8YSDI4.sentenceLength: Insert a sharp, emotionally resonant quote that captures the tension and urgency of the moment.
8YSDI4.description: Make it emotionally resonant and specific. Use one believable line that sounds like something a real executive would actually say.
examples: We spend more time debating what we're working on than actually working on it — and I still can't tell you if we're working on the right things.
I have seventeen dashboards and none of them tell me what I actually need to know before the next board meeting.
Our planning process is sophisticated, thorough, and completely disconnected from what the teams are actually doing by week three of the quarter.
We keep hiring more program managers to manage the coordination load instead of fixing the system that creates it.

BFA5OX.sentenceLength: Insert a reframing statement that challenges the assumptions underlying their current tradeoffs
BFA5OX.description: Be concrete and operational. Name the real mechanism causing the pain, not just a symptom word like alignment or visibility.
examples: the coordination overhead required to stay strategically coherent didn't have to scale with organizational complexity
you didn't have to choose between giving teams autonomy and knowing where your capital is actually going
planning and execution didn't have to live in separate systems, maintained by separate teams, producing separate versions of the truth
portfolio-level visibility didn't require a dedicated reporting function and a three-week data collection cycle

K9SQNG.sentenceLength: Insert two commonly perceived tradeoffs, e.g., “control and autonomy,” “flexibility and structure,” “visibility and team independence”
K9SQNG.description: Pick two genuinely competing priorities and keep both sides parallel and specific.
examples: control and autonomy
flexibility and structure
visibility and team independence
strategic coherence and execution speed
centralized oversight and decentralized decision-making

SASM3A.sentenceLength: Describe a new outcome possibility that directly mirrors their challenges
SASM3A.description: Be concrete and operational. Name the real mechanism causing the pain, not just a symptom word like alignment or visibility.
examples: real-time portfolio clarity across every active initiative — with no manual aggregation
planning cycles cut from six weeks to two, with no loss of strategic coherence
teams operating autonomously within clear constraints, with leaders able to see trade-offs in context before they become incidents
a single shared record of what was decided, why, and how resources moved as a result — accessible to everyone who needs it

NNIBKH.sentenceLength: Describe the downside currently associated with that outcome — such as overhead, complexity, political friction, cost, or rigidity
NNIBKH.description: Be concrete and operational. Name the real mechanism causing the pain, not just a symptom word like alignment or visibility.

complexity

HZE7RI.sentenceLength: Describe the big mission — concrete, ambitious, but grounded in business impact
HZE7RI.description: Anchor the response in measurable business impact. Prefer specific outcomes over abstract aspirations.
examples: make the gap between strategy and execution visible, navigable, and closeable — for every team in every company
give every organization the operating infrastructure to make and revise resource decisions at the speed their market demands
replace the coordination tax with a coordination advantage — where the act of working across teams generates insight instead of consuming it
build the connective tissue between strategy, capital, and execution that most companies are currently trying to stitch together with spreadsheets and slide decks

EGQLZF.sentenceLength: Describe a short story of the improved state — how teams operate, how leaders make decisions, what changes behaviorally and structurally
EGQLZF.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: product and engineering leaders look at the same dashboard every Monday — not because someone spent Friday pulling data, but because the system maintains that view continuously. When a priority shifts, every affected team sees the downstream implication immediately, and the decision that caused the shift is right there in context.

quarterly planning takes four days instead of six weeks, because the baseline is always current. Leaders spend their time on trade-offs, not on reconciling numbers. Teams walk out of planning knowing exactly what they are committing to and why.

a CPO can walk into a board meeting and show exactly how capital is moving across the portfolio — which bets are funded, which are starved, and what the expected return is on each — without a single spreadsheet being prepared the night before.

H5DBQ7.sentenceLength: Describe a realistic obstacle — technical feasibility, adoption risk, category fatigue, legacy lock-in, etc.
H5DBQ7.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: legacy data models in existing tools meant the structured data our approach needed did not exist yet — it had to be captured from scratch
category fatigue — executives had heard the 'single source of truth' pitch from too many vendors to take it seriously without proof
adoption risk was real: the workflows we needed to change were deeply embedded in how people earned credit and visibility
the skeptics argued that any centralized system would become a compliance burden rather than a decision tool, and they had enough prior evidence to make that case credibly

P8ZV1J.sentenceLength: Explain why the challenge seemed surmountable — technological shifts, market timing, new approaches, customer validation, etc.
P8ZV1J.description: Be concrete and operational. Name the real mechanism causing the pain, not just a symptom word like alignment or visibility.
examples: the shift toward continuous planning — accelerated by the pandemic — had already broken the annual planning model for most companies, creating genuine demand for something better
a new generation of data infrastructure made it possible to keep a structured portfolio model current without a dedicated team maintaining it
our first design partners were already trying to solve this with internally-built tools, which proved the appetite was real and the market was not serving it
AI made it tractable to surface the right context at the right moment — something that previously required a human analyst embedded in every planning conversation

SGM0OU.sentenceLength: Insert a positioning statement using language familiar to the market, even if imperfect — e.g., “AI-native operating model platform,” “strategic portfolio system,” etc.

48WZ8Y.sentenceLength: Explain what that word specifically means in your context — not the buzzword definition
48WZ8Y.description: Choose a trend the audience already recognizes and connect it to their actual operating context.
examples: not that we bolt AI onto existing workflows — we mean the system is designed from the ground up around machine-readable context, so AI can reason across your portfolio without needing a human to pre-structure the query
not the org chart or the RACI matrix — we mean the actual system of decisions, resources, and accountabilities that determines how work gets prioritized and capital gets deployed
not high-level goal-setting, but the active management of trade-offs: what to fund, what to delay, what to stop, and how those decisions ripple through the organization
not the financial portfolio — we mean the full set of active work investments the company is making, treated as a coherent whole rather than a sum of independent team backlogs

VSVH6A.sentenceLength: Insert the second word in your positioning label

not an analytics layer that surfaces patterns after the fact — we mean active, in-context guidance that helps leaders make better decisions before they compound into problems
not a set of reports or dashboards — we mean a structured, shared representation of how the organization is actually operating, updated continuously and queryable by anyone who needs it

the organizational scar tissue they create — each one adds process weight and reduces the team's ability to move
the siloed data models that prevent them from producing a coherent view without a human analyst bridging the gaps

1RWH1S.sentenceLength: List tools or processes that become redundant, simplified, or redefined
1RWH1S.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: manual quarterly planning decks, siloed headcount trackers, and the rotation of status-report spreadsheets that nobody trusts
the Excel capacity model, the slide-based roadmap, and the ad-hoc Slack threads where real prioritization decisions actually happen
the weekly status call that exists solely because there is no shared operational record — and the reporting analyst role that supports it
duplicated data entry across Jira, Workday, and finance systems that currently produces three different answers to the same question

9PHPHL.sentenceLength: Insert a specific persona, e.g., “Product Operations Lead”

Head of Planning and Portfolio Management

PVGS4C.sentenceLength: Describe concretely how they interact with the product — workflows, actions, responsibilities
PVGS4C.description: Be persona-specific: describe concrete workflows, decisions, and responsibilities.
examples: build and maintain the operational model — mapping active initiatives to teams, capacity, and strategic priorities — so leaders always have a current, structured view of what the organization is actually doing
run the planning cycle: collecting capacity commitments, surfacing conflicts, and producing a shared plan that teams can actually execute against
manage the signal layer — tracking where plans are drifting from commitments and escalating the ones that need a decision before they become a crisis
configure the taxonomy that makes portfolio data comparable across teams, and own the governance process that keeps it current

LKUCN6.sentenceLength: Describe their job to be done
LKUCN6.description: Be persona-specific: describe concrete workflows, decisions, and responsibilities.
examples: eliminate the two to three weeks of manual data collection that currently precede every planning conversation
give senior leadership a real-time view of portfolio health without creating a dedicated reporting function to maintain it
make the planning process less of a negotiation and more of a structured decision — with shared data and explicit trade-offs visible to everyone in the room
build the operational infrastructure that lets the company scale its planning discipline without scaling the team that runs it

3U1M78.description: Be persona-specific: describe concrete workflows, decisions, and responsibilities.
examples: Chief Product Officer
VP of Engineering
Head of Strategy and Operations
SVP of Product and Technology

WYJTM2.sentenceLength: Describe how they perceive or position it internally
WYJTM2.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: their command center — the one place where they can see what the organization is committed to, what is at risk, and what trade-offs are in front of them, before those trade-offs make the decision for them
the system that finally makes the quarterly planning conversation a genuine strategic discussion rather than a data reconciliation exercise
the infrastructure that makes autonomous teams and portfolio-level coherence compatible — not a choice between them
the thing that lets them walk into a board meeting with confidence instead of caveats

JHJYK3.description: Be persona-specific: describe concrete workflows, decisions, and responsibilities.
examples: less about configuration and more about consumption — they check the portfolio view at the start of the week, flag the items that need a decision, and use the system to communicate the context behind those decisions to their direct reports and peers
they use the exception view to scan for capacity conflicts and stalled initiatives, and they use the decision log to maintain a shared record of what was changed and why
they engage primarily through the briefing layer — a synthesized view of what is moving, what is stuck, and what needs their attention — rather than digging into the underlying data themselves

GZ4VHW.description: Anchor the response in measurable business impact. Prefer specific outcomes over abstract aspirations.
examples: the confidence to make and communicate resource decisions quickly, with enough context that teams understand the reasoning and can execute without a follow-up meeting
a coherent story for the board about how capital is moving across the portfolio and what the expected return is — without needing an analyst to build the deck the night before
the ability to run portfolio reviews that surface the right trade-offs instead of relitigating the same prioritization debates cycle after cycle
visibility into which teams are set up to succeed and which are structurally under-resourced — before it shows up in the quarterly results

U3AJ08.sentenceLength: Reiterate your brand promise clearly and concretely.
U3AJ08.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: Strategy and execution, finally in the same room.
Know where your capital is going. Know what it is producing. Change course before it is too late.
The operating clarity your organization needs to move fast without losing coherence.
Give every leader the context to make good decisions. Give every team the clarity to execute them.

GRKGMP.sentenceLength: core feature status quo focuses on

examples: feature-level project tracking
static quarterly roadmaps

SXNORH.sentenceLength: the sacrifice of shifting to Dotwork
SXNORH.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: the pixel-level Gantt chart and the granular task dependency view
deep per-ticket reporting and individual velocity tracking
the ability to manage at the task level — Dotwork operates above the level of individual work items
the kind of configuration flexibility that requires a dedicated admin to maintain

T3XSVG.description: Anchor the response in measurable business impact. Prefer specific outcomes over abstract aspirations.
examples: leaders gain portfolio-level clarity without the overhead of a dedicated reporting function
the organization can move faster at the strategic level while teams retain the autonomy to determine how they execute
planning becomes a continuous process rather than a quarterly event — with decisions captured in context and visible to everyone who needs them
the coordination layer costs a fraction of what it costs today, and produces a much clearer signal

N5YRK5.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: deep task-level granularity and individual contributor tracking
out-of-the-box integrations with every niche tool category
highly configurable workflow automation for specific team rituals
advanced Gantt and timeline visualization for project-level scheduling

8886JY.sentenceLength: contrary outcome or pattern that makes deprioritizing that rational
8886JY.description: Anchor the response in measurable business impact. Prefer specific outcomes over abstract aspirations.
examples: the teams that get the most value from Dotwork are the ones who have already given up on task-level tracking as a management tool — they operate at the initiative and outcome level, and that is exactly where our model shines
customers consistently tell us they tried to solve this with more granular tracking before finding us, and the overhead of maintaining that granularity was itself a source of coordination drag
the organizations most frustrated by our limitations in this area are also the ones least likely to benefit from what we do well — we have learned to treat that as a useful filter

4VE9VJ.sentenceLength: Increase or decrease a known cost or investment driver — for example, reduce reporting overhead, increase capital allocation clarity, shorten planning cycles
4VE9VJ.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: reduce reporting overhead
increase capital allocation clarity
shorten planning cycles
reduce the time between a strategic decision and its reflection in team-level plans
cut the number of people required to produce and maintain the portfolio view

YPW31K.sentenceLength: Describe what changes downstream — decision speed, prioritization accuracy, cross-team coordination, etc.
YPW31K.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: decision speed at the portfolio level — leaders can see trade-offs in context and make calls without waiting for a data pull
prioritization accuracy — teams are working on the things that are actually most important, not the things that were committed to in a planning cycle three months ago
cross-team coordination quality — dependencies are surfaced earlier, and the teams managing them have shared context instead of conflicting assumptions
the planning conversation itself — it shifts from data reconciliation to genuine strategic trade-off discussion

A39M53.sentenceLength: Describe final business impact — improved capital efficiency, faster time to value, reduced waste, increased strategic coherence, etc.
A39M53.description: Anchor the response in measurable business impact. Prefer specific outcomes over abstract aspirations.
examples: a measurable improvement in capital efficiency — fewer resources deployed against low-leverage work, more against the initiatives that actually move the needle
faster time to value for strategic priorities — because the gap between decision and execution is shorter and less lossy
reduced strategic drift — the organization stays coherent as it scales, rather than fragmenting into independent operating units with no shared model of what matters
a durable competitive advantage in execution speed — the ability to reallocate capital and redirect effort faster than competitors still running annual planning cycles

VDZJ71.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: quarterly planning decks, spreadsheet-based capacity models, and weekly status reports
Jira for execution, Workday for headcount, Excel for the planning model, and slides for the narrative
OKR platforms, project management tools, and BI dashboards — none of which talk to each other
a combination of governance rituals, reporting layers, and PMO functions that together approximate what a shared operating model should provide

FU3BDT.sentenceLength: State their ultimate goal — e.g., strategic clarity, capital efficiency, execution confidence
FU3BDT.description: Anchor the response in measurable business impact. Prefer specific outcomes over abstract aspirations.
examples: strategic clarity
capital efficiency
execution confidence
portfolio coherence at scale
the ability to move faster than their market without losing strategic alignment

Y6OTI5.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: traditional project management tools
OKR platforms that capture goals but not the resource decisions behind them
quarterly planning decks maintained by a central ops team
resource management software built for project-based delivery work

WOJF67.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: maintains a live, structured model of your entire portfolio — not a snapshot, not a dashboard, but a continuously updated operating record
connects the 'what we decided' to the 'what we are doing' in a single system, so the gap between strategy and execution becomes visible and auditable
gives leaders portfolio-level clarity without requiring a dedicated team to aggregate, clean, and present data every week
treats planning as a continuous process rather than a periodic event — so when strategy shifts, the system updates rather than starting over

3WL09C.description: Anchor the response in measurable business impact. Prefer specific outcomes over abstract aspirations.
examples: make and revise strategic commitments at the speed your market demands — without losing operational coherence
run your planning process in days instead of weeks, with better data and fewer surprises
give every leader the context to make good decisions without adding headcount to produce the clarity they need
move from reactive portfolio management to deliberate capital allocation — with a live model that makes the cost of every trade-off visible

A7BO1B.sentenceLength: Describe the fundamental architectural or philosophical shift
A7BO1B.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: modeling the operating layer as a structured graph of decisions, resources, and outcomes rather than a hierarchy of tasks and milestones
building the system around the decision — not the project. Every piece of data in Dotwork is attached to the choice that created it, so the reasoning is never lost.
treating the portfolio model as the product — a living artifact that the whole organization reads from and writes to, rather than a deliverable produced by a central team
starting from the question 'what decisions need to be made?' rather than 'what work needs to be tracked?' — which changes everything about the data model

5WRDBC.description: Be concrete and operational. Name the real mechanism causing the pain, not just a symptom word like alignment or visibility.
examples: data models designed for project tracking that make it structurally difficult to reason about initiatives, outcomes, and capital in the same system
architecture built for individual team use that cannot aggregate coherently without a purpose-built reporting layer on top
a product philosophy centered on task-level fidelity that makes portfolio-level abstraction feel bolted-on rather than native
years of customer commitments to existing feature sets that constrain how radically they can rethink the underlying model

3F9YXW.sentenceLength: Describe specific architectural or design freedoms you've used
3F9YXW.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: build the data model from scratch around portfolio decisions rather than adapting a project model upward
design the user experience for the executive and ops audience from day one, rather than building for individual contributors and trying to add a leadership layer later
treat AI not as a feature but as a first-class design constraint — every data structure in the system was built to be machine-readable and queryable from the start

make integration the default rather than the exception — we assumed from the beginning that we would need to connect to Jira, Workday, and finance systems, and we built the data model to support that cleanly

D6FIN2.sentenceLength: State a distinctive, potentially controversial viewpoint
D6FIN2.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: most organizations do not have a strategy problem — they have a translation problem. The strategy exists. The execution exists. There is just no system connecting them.
the coordination overhead most companies accept as a cost of doing business is actually a design flaw — and it is solvable at the infrastructure level, not the process level

OKRs, quarterly planning, and portfolio reviews are all attempts to solve the same underlying problem — none of them quite work because they are operating on top of an architecture that was not designed for strategic coherence
adding more headcount to manage coordination is the organizational equivalent of buying a bigger hard drive when what you need is a database

W8CHUM.sentenceLength: State the underlying principle driving your approach
W8CHUM.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: if you can see the gap between your intent and your execution clearly enough, you can close it — most organizations cannot see it, which is why it persists
good decisions at the portfolio level require shared context, not shared opinions — the system should provide the context so the conversation can be about the trade-off, not the data
the organizations that will win over the next decade are not the ones with the best strategy — they are the ones who can move capital and attention to the right place faster than their competitors
autonomy and coherence are not in tension. They are only in tension when the system does not exist to support both simultaneously.

SN0RT2.sentenceLength: Describe a customer's industry, size, and operating context
SN0RT2.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: a 1,200-person B2B SaaS company in the HR tech space, running a multi-product portfolio across three business units after a series of acquisitions
a 600-person FinTech scale-up preparing for a Series D, with a product org that had grown faster than its planning infrastructure
a professional services firm with 800 people and a portfolio of 40+ active client engagements, struggling to allocate shared resources across competing priorities
a mid-market SaaS company with 400 engineers across eight product teams, each running their own planning process with no shared model of cross-team capacity

0MUYBO.sentenceLength: Reframe their problem in concrete terms
0MUYBO.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: an inability to see where their 200-person engineering organization was actually spending its time — not because the data did not exist, but because it lived in four different systems with no reconciliation layer
a planning process that consumed eight weeks of leadership time per quarter and still produced a plan that teams stopped following by week three of execution
the structural impossibility of answering 'what are we actually working on and why?' without a multi-week data collection exercise
a 40% gap between the strategic priorities the executive team believed they were funding and the work their teams were actually doing

7FB7UQ.description: Anchor the response in measurable business impact. Prefer specific outcomes over abstract aspirations.
examples: a 60% reduction in planning cycle time and a 35% improvement in on-strategy delivery within two quarters
full portfolio visibility in under 24 hours — a process that previously required two weeks of manual data collection
three planning cycles run in four days each, down from the six-week process they had been running for two years
a 25% reduction in misallocated engineering capacity within one quarter of deployment

RVX30X.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: Dotwork's portfolio model and automated data sync with their existing Jira and Workday instances
the initiative mapping layer and continuous capacity tracking, which replaced their quarterly spreadsheet process
the decision log and trade-off visualization, which gave their leadership team a shared operating record for the first time
the cross-team dependency graph and resource allocation dashboard, which surfaced conflicts two to three weeks earlier than their previous process

MA3BNX.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: a 900-person digital health company scaling from startup to enterprise across three product lines
a publicly traded infrastructure software company with a complex multi-segment portfolio and a recent leadership change
a 500-person marketplace platform operating in a high-velocity market with frequent strategic pivots
a Series C SaaS company in the legal tech space with a product org growing faster than its operational infrastructure

KFEYSX.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: a planning process that required seventeen people for six weeks and still produced a plan nobody trusted
an inability to answer basic portfolio questions — what is funded, what is not, what changed and why — without days of manual aggregation
a 'single source of truth' that existed in theory but was maintained by one person who was also the single point of failure for every planning cycle
three separate portfolio views maintained by finance, product, and engineering — each correct from its own angle, none compatible with the others

BQAX81.description: Anchor the response in measurable business impact. Prefer specific outcomes over abstract aspirations.
examples: a 40% reduction in coordination overhead, measured by the hours their ops team spent aggregating planning data each quarter
the ability to run their quarterly planning process in one week rather than six, with better cross-team alignment than they had achieved in previous cycles
a 30% improvement in on-strategy execution within two quarters, attributable to the shared portfolio model replacing conflicting team-level roadmaps
elimination of the quarterly data reconciliation phase that previously consumed the first three weeks of every planning cycle

WHGFOJ.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: the portfolio snapshot and automated weekly digest, which replaced the manual status report cycle
the initiative-to-team mapping and capacity tracking layer, which made the capacity model self-maintaining
the scenario planning and trade-off view, which transformed their quarterly planning conversation from a data exercise into a strategic discussion
the integration layer that unified Jira, Workday, and their financial planning system into a single operational record

NSJBXE.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: a 700-person B2B software company in the compliance and risk space, operating across five geographic regions with distinct planning cadences
a late-stage startup building infrastructure for the logistics industry, with 350 people and a portfolio diversified across two market segments
a 1,500-person enterprise software company managing a portfolio of 90+ active initiatives across four product divisions
a mid-market SaaS company that had tripled headcount in eighteen months and was operating its planning process the same way it had when it had 80 people

OK45FP.description: Be concrete and operational. Name the real mechanism causing the pain, not just a symptom word like alignment or visibility.
examples: a governance process that added four to six weeks of latency to every strategic reallocation — making it structurally impossible to respond to market shifts within a planning quarter
a portfolio of 90 active initiatives with no shared model of relative priority — meaning every resource conversation started from scratch
a leadership team that was technically aligned on strategy but had no shared operating record of what that strategy meant for how resources were actually deployed
the political overhead of managing cross-team priorities without a neutral, data-grounded system — every prioritization conversation became a negotiation between departments

T24XK0.description: Anchor the response in measurable business impact. Prefer specific outcomes over abstract aspirations.
examples: a 50% reduction in the time from strategic decision to team-level execution, from an average of six weeks to under three
the first portfolio review in three years that required no pre-work from the operations team — the data was already current
elimination of the quarterly reforecast exercise, saving approximately 400 hours of leadership and ops time per year
a 20% improvement in capital allocation accuracy, measured by the delta between funded priorities and end-of-quarter delivered outcomes

B44BCD.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: Dotwork's live portfolio model and the governance workflow, which automated the approval and communication of strategic reallocations
the operating model graph, which gave each product division a shared view of cross-team dependencies and funding decisions
the briefing layer and decision log, which replaced the weekly leadership status meeting with an asynchronous operating record
the scenario modeling and initiative prioritization tools, which gave the leadership team a structured way to evaluate trade-offs before committing to a reallocation

YG8ITL.sentenceLength: insert % estimate or rough dollar range of waste / inefficiency / misallocated capital they might plausibly be experiencing
YG8ITL.description: Use credible numbers and defensible ranges. Avoid inflated claims.
examples: 10% of your annual engineering spend
$2–5M in misallocated capital
15–20% of your leadership team's available time per quarter
three to four planning cycles' worth of rework per year

Q2JFRT.sentenceLength: describe the specific friction you outlined earlier — e.g., duplicated planning effort, stalled initiatives, unclear capital allocation, coordination overhead
Q2JFRT.description: Be concrete and operational. Name the real mechanism causing the pain, not just a symptom word like alignment or visibility.
examples: duplicated planning effort
stalled initiatives
unclear capital allocation
coordination overhead
the gap between what leadership believes is funded and what teams are actually working on

CS8BL2.sentenceLength: insert meaningful but believable annual dollar impact range
CS8BL2.description: Anchor the response in measurable business impact. Prefer specific outcomes over abstract aspirations.
examples: $3–8M in recovered capacity and reduced coordination overhead annually
$5–12M in improved capital allocation and faster time to value on strategic priorities
$1.5–4M in planning overhead alone, before accounting for the downstream cost of executing against the wrong priorities
a meaningful fraction of your annual engineering budget — typically 8–15% when misaligned priorities are properly accounted for

BYIQPM.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: mid-market B2B SaaS companies with 300–1,500 employees
multi-product technology companies managing complex portfolios across multiple business units
high-growth scale-ups where the planning infrastructure has not kept pace with organizational complexity
enterprise software companies where the coordination cost of strategic alignment has become a structural tax on execution speed

CY7YC6.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: quarterly planning in slides and spreadsheets
OKR platforms that capture goals but not the resource decisions behind them
manual reporting cycles maintained by a central ops team
a combination of Jira, Workday, and Excel that approximates a portfolio model without actually being one

SLH36N.sentenceLength: describe mechanism — slower decisions, lower throughput, hidden coordination tax, misaligned investments
SLH36N.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: slower decisions, because the context required to make a good call takes longer to assemble than the window in which the decision is relevant

hidden coordination tax — the hours spent in alignment meetings, status calls, and data reconciliation that do not appear on any balance sheet but accumulate into a significant drag on execution speed
misaligned investments that compound quarter over quarter, as teams continue to optimize for local priorities that have drifted from the organization's strategic intent
lower throughput on strategic priorities, because the best people are pulled into the planning and coordination process rather than the work itself

PV7418.sentenceLength: insert short, contained engagement format — e.g., “4–6 week operating model diagnostic,” “single-quarter pilot,” “one-domain proof of value,” “capacity allocation sprint”
PV7418.description: Keep it bounded and practical: narrow scope, clear owner set, and low-friction inputs.
examples: 4–6 week operating model diagnostic
single-quarter pilot
one-domain proof of value
capacity allocation sprint

N16QIB.sentenceLength: narrow use case tied to their pain
N16QIB.description: Keep it specific, evidence-aware, and easy for an executive reader to validate quickly.
examples: one product area or business unit's quarterly planning cycle
the cross-team dependency and capacity tracking problem for their two most resource-constrained teams
the leadership portfolio review process — replacing the six-week data collection with a live operating model
a single planning quarter, run end-to-end in Dotwork alongside their existing process so the comparison is direct

ONO9F3.sentenceLength: insert concrete metric we can improve
ONO9F3.description: Use credible numbers and defensible ranges. Avoid inflated claims.
examples: time spent on data collection and reconciliation before each planning cycle
the delta between funded priorities and delivered outcomes at end of quarter
hours of leadership time consumed by status reporting and coordination meetings per week
lead time from strategic decision to team-level execution

QQUC8S.description: Use credible numbers and defensible ranges. Avoid inflated claims.
examples: the dollar value of engineering capacity deployed against low-priority or misaligned initiatives
the cost of the coordination infrastructure currently required to maintain portfolio visibility
the planning overhead — in hours and headcount — required to run one complete planning cycle
the frequency and cost of strategic reallocations that happened too late to prevent a downstream impact

G18IIG.sentenceLength: insert strategic outcome they care about
G18IIG.description: Anchor the response in measurable business impact. Prefer specific outcomes over abstract aspirations.
examples: a planning process the organization can trust and execute against
portfolio-level clarity without the overhead of a dedicated reporting function
the ability to reallocate capital and redirect effort at the speed their market demands
a shared operating model that scales with the organization rather than requiring more coordination infrastructure to maintain

W2VTJC.description: Keep it bounded and practical: narrow scope, clear owner set, and low-friction inputs.
examples: the head of Product Operations and one or two senior product leaders
the CPO and VP of Engineering, plus the ops or PMO lead who currently runs the planning process
four to six stakeholders: one from finance, one from product, one from engineering, and the person currently responsible for maintaining the portfolio view
the leadership team and the two or three people closest to the current planning pain

CQUNJK.description: Keep it bounded and practical: narrow scope, clear owner set, and low-friction inputs.
examples: one business unit and one planning cycle
the portfolio review process — not the day-to-day execution tracking
initiatives above a defined size threshold — typically anything consuming more than two engineers for more than one quarter
the cross-team resource allocation problem — not individual team backlog management

Z2VHTH.description: Be concrete and operational. Name the real mechanism causing the pain, not just a symptom word like alignment or visibility.
examples: a list of active initiatives with rough size and team ownership — most companies have this in some form already
read access to existing Jira projects and Workday headcount data, which the integration layer can sync automatically
a one-time initiative inventory exercise — typically a two-hour working session with the ops lead and two to three product managers
a structured export from their existing planning tool, or access to the spreadsheet model they are currently maintaining manually

JDUGAF.sentenceLength: insert credible dollar range — e.g., “$15–40k depending on scope” or “roughly the cost of one mid-level hire for a month”
JDUGAF.description: Keep it bounded and practical: narrow scope, clear owner set, and low-friction inputs.
examples: $15–40k depending on scope
roughly the cost of one mid-level hire for a month

8EHGS7.sentenceLength: tangible artifact 1 — e.g., clarity on structural constraints
8EHGS7.description: Be concrete and operational. Name the real mechanism causing the pain, not just a symptom word like alignment or visibility.
examples: clarity on structural constraints
a documented map of where the coordination overhead is actually coming from
an honest assessment of whether your current planning process is fixable or needs to be replaced

XYA5B7.sentenceLength: tangible artifact 2 — e.g., quantified opportunity map
XYA5B7.description: Use credible numbers and defensible ranges. Avoid inflated claims.

BQ0XYC.sentenceLength: tangible artifact 3 — e.g., refined operating hypotheses
